example_prompt: Receive a refund.completed webhook from Flutterwave, verify the HMAC-SHA256 signature, then mark the associated order as refunded.

type.description: Event type. Always "refund.completed" for this webhook.

webhook_id.description: Unique webhook event id (e.g. "wh_abc123"). Use it to deduplicate retried deliveries.

timestamp.description: Unix epoch timestamp (int64) when the event was emitted (e.g. 1701619661).

charge_id.description: Id of the original charge being refunded.

amount_refunded.description: Amount refunded (may be less than the original charge for partial refunds).

reason.description: Reason that was supplied when the refund was created.

response_schema.description: Your endpoint must respond with HTTP 200 and an (optionally empty) JSON body within 60 seconds.

error_schema.description: Webhooks do not return errors to Flutterwave. Any non-2xx response triggers retries.

gotchas: Flutterwave signs webhook payloads with HMAC-SHA256 using the secret hash configured in your dashboard. Compute HMAC-SHA256 of the raw request body and compare with the 'flutterwave-signature' header. Reject mismatches. Respond with HTTP 200 within 60 seconds — Flutterwave retries failed webhooks 3 times at 30-minute intervals.

The top-level event id field is 'webhook_id' for refund.completed (not 'id'), and 'timestamp' is a Unix epoch integer (not an ISO 8601 string). Flutterwave is inconsistent across event types — charge.completed historically uses 'id'.

data.status is broader than just 'completed' — it can be pending | requires_action | succeeded | failed | cancelled | completed | new. Always check the status explicitly before marking a refund as final.
Use the raw request body (bytes/string) when computing HMAC — re-serialising parsed JSON will reorder keys and break verification.
Use a constant-time comparison (e.g. node:crypto timingSafeEqual) when comparing signatures to avoid timing-side-channel attacks.
Deduplicate on webhook_id — Flutterwave may redeliver the same event after a transient failure on your side.
Always verify the refund server-side via GET /refunds/{id} before adjusting customer ledgers — never trust webhook data alone.
